chunks.title: Course Information / Course Description / Special Course Designation / Collaboration
chunks.content:
Meeting Times: Meeting Type: LEC
Meeting Days: TR
Start Time: 09:35AM
End Time: 10:50AM
Start Date: 08/25/2025
End Date: 12/16/2025
Meeting Location: EABB 106

Credit Hours: 3

Machine learning is a field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed. It is the core of AI. In the past decade, machine learning has created tremendous value with its application in various domains, including information retrieval, speech recognition, computer vision, understanding of human genome, natural language processing, etc. In this class, students will not only learn the fundamental machine learning techniques, but also gain the hands-on experience by applying these techniques and developing new techniques for solving problems from the real world. In addition, students will learn some recent advances in deep learning and AI. Topics of interest include: (1) Regression (least square regression, lasso). (2) Classification (support vector machines, logistic regression). (3) Kernel Methods. (4) Unsupervised methods; (5) Self-supervised learning; (6) Stochastic Optimization. (7) Deep Learning. By the end of class, students are expected to accomplish a project related to machine learning in a group.

None

Both homework assignments and projects are team-based, and each student must join a team of 2–3 members to collaborate. Only one copy of the homework or project needs to be submitted per team, and every submission must clearly acknowledge each member's contributions. If a student is found to be uncooperative, they may be removed from the team and required to form or join a new team if necessary.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content:
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): CSCE 420 or CSCE 625.

• You should be comfortable with some Math and Programming.
• Math Background: Linear algebra, Calculus, Probability and Statistics
• Programming Background: Python, Pytorch
• Preferably access to GPUs (e.g., Google Colab).

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

• Have a good understanding of basic machine learning methods.
• Know how to train a ML model

chunks.title: Grading Policy / Grading Scale
chunks.content:
• Homework assignments (six homework): 40%
• Exams (two mid-term exams): 30%
• Final Project: 30% (Presentation: 5%, Code: 10%, Report: 15%)

A = [90, 100]
B = [80, 90)
C = [70, 80)
D = [60, 70)
F = <60

chunks.title: Late Work Policy / Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

Late submission caused by excused absence (defined in Student rule 7) will be accepted. Excused absence will not be counted towards late days. Contact the instructor if you think you have an extremely rare circumstance for which we should make an exception. This policy is to ensure that feedback can be given in a timely manner.

Unexpected excused absences: In cases where prior notification of excused absence is not feasible (e.g., accident or emergency) the student must provide notification by the end of the second working day after the absence, including an explanation of why notice could not be sent prior to the class.

Each homework assignment due by midnight (11:59pm) on the due date. Late homeworks will not be graded so please just hand in whatever you have completed. Late project submissions will not be accepted.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content: The use of AI tools such as large language models for completing homework assignments, exams, or the course project is strictly prohibited. However, it is acceptable to use them for tasks such as editing your writing or assisting in debugging your code.
